title: Providing for consideration of the bill (H.R. 27) to amend the Controlled Substances Act with respect to the scheduling of fentanyl-related substances, and for other purposes.
plain_summary: This House resolution sets up the rules for discussing a bill about fentanyl drugs. The bill would change how the government classifies dangerous fentanyl-like substances under drug laws. The resolution lets the House of Representatives skip most of the usual long procedures and move quickly to vote on the bill. There will only be a short time for debate and very few chances to make changes to the bill before voting.
last_action_text: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

sections.title: House Resolution 93
sections.text: The House of Representatives passed this resolution on February fifth, two thousand twenty five. When this resolution is approved, the House can consider bill H.R. twenty seven. This bill would change the Controlled Substances Act (the main law that controls illegal drugs) regarding how fentanyl-related substances are classified. Any rules that would normally prevent the House from discussing this bill are set aside. One specific change printed in part A of the Rules Committee report will automatically be included in the bill. The bill with this change will be treated as if everyone has already read it. Any rules that would normally block parts of the amended bill are also set aside. The House will move directly to a final vote on the bill without other motions getting in the way, except for three things. First, there will be one hour of debate split equally between the chair and top minority member of the Energy and Commerce Committee or people they choose to speak for them. Second, if the designated member offers it, there can be one more change printed in part B of the Rules Committee report. This change cannot be blocked by procedural rules, will be treated as read, can be debated for the time listed in the report with time split between someone supporting it and someone opposing it, and cannot be split into separate votes. Third, there can be one motion to send the bill back to committee.
